author: Zestyclose_Humor3362
text:
That's such a weird coincidence. I remember reading that Matt Groening named a bunch of characters after his own family - Homer was his dad, Marge and Lisa were his mom and sister. Even the street they live on, Evergreen Terrace, was the name of the street he grew up on in Portland.

The whole Simpsons naming thing is full of these little stories. Like how Bart was supposed to reveal he was an alien in the series finale that Groening planned when he thought the show would only last a few seasons. And Milhouse got his name from Richard Nixon's middle name because they thought it was the most unfortunate name for a kid to have.

author: Samtyang
text:
1. My grandpa officiated high school wrestling for 45 years and only stopped at 79 because his knees gave out.. not his eyesight or anything, just couldn't kneel down for the mat work anymore

2. The crazy part is these older refs usually know every single rule better than the young guys. Like they've seen literally every weird situation that can happen in a game

3. I wonder if he started when he was like 20... that means he's been calling games since the early 60s. Think about all the rule changes he's had to learn over the decades

4. There's this 78 year old ump in our softball league who still sprints to first base on close calls. Puts some of the 40-somethings to shame

5. Basketball reffing at 83 sounds exhausting though. All that running up and down the court

author: Billy1121
text:
For context, Medicaid pays for longer nursing home stays while Medicare does not. So many seniors have to go on Medicaid in their later years to afford long term nursing home care.

Different states go about it in different ways. This article refers to Iowa which uses a third party vendor who gets 11% of all recoveries, so they recovered about $29 million in 2019. Whereas Hawaii has about half the population of Iowa and only recovered around $30,000.

Depending on the state, retirement accounts or homes may be included or excluded. Iowa is brutal because in this article they even went after back pay because the daughter of an elderly person unwittingly enrolled in a program to pay caregivers, which was (unknown to her) actually a Medicaid program.

> "There's a discrimination against what I call 'the wrong type of disease,'" Einhart says. Medicare could spend hundreds of thousands of dollars on hospital treatment for a person with serious heart problems or cancer, and no government representatives would try to recoup the money from the person's estate. But people with other conditions, such as dementia, often need extended nursing home care, which Medicare won't cover. Many such patients wind up on Medicaid, and their estates are billed.
